A Practical Guide · 2026

Introduction to Artificial Intelligence

From transformers to practical workflows
Browse Sections
Section 01

Introduction

Artificial intelligence is the most significant technological shift since the internet. If you haven't started using it yet you're not too late — but we're approaching the point where not using it means missing out.

To understand what AI actually is — and why it suddenly got so powerful — it helps to start with how computers have always worked and why that approach eventually hit a wall.

Brief Timeline

  • 1950sConcept of machine learning first proposed
  • 1980s–90sNeural networks explored but limited by computing power — a period later dubbed the "AI winter"
  • 2012Deep learning breakthrough in image recognition
  • 2013DeepMind's AI teaches itself to play Atari games
  • 2016AlphaGo beats world champion at Go (Move 37)
  • 2017The transformer architecture invented (the big one)
  • 2020Scaling laws formally described
  • 2022ChatGPT launched, bringing AI to the mainstream
  • 2024–25Reasoning models, multimodal AI, agents
The pace of development is accelerating — not slowing down.
The AI models you use today are the worst AI models you will ever use.
Section 02

How Traditional Software Worked

Before we get to AI, it helps to understand how most software was built for decades — and why that approach eventually hit a wall.

Software Just Follows Instructions

Traditional software has no understanding or judgement of its own. It does exactly what it's told — extremely fast and extremely precisely.

Traditional software is like a giant recipe. The computer follows the rules a human has written — perfectly, exactly, and without question. But if those rules are wrong, incomplete, or don't cover the situation, the software cannot adapt.

For example:

  • IF the password matches → THEN grant access
  • IF the balance is too low → THEN decline the transaction
  • IF the email contains "Nigerian prince" → THEN move to spam

The software will follow these instructions exactly — even if the rule contains a mistake.

Imagine a Recipe That Says…

"Add 10 tablespoons of salt."

A human cook would probably realise something is wrong. Traditional software would simply follow the instruction exactly, because it has no common sense or understanding of the bigger picture.

Why This Hits a Wall

Rules work brilliantly for structured problems — banking systems, calculators, traffic lights. But try writing rules to recognise a cat in a photo.

The Cat Problem

"Four legs and pointy ears" also describes a fox. "Whiskers" fits mice and seals. What about:

  • a cat photographed from behind?
  • a black cat in a dark room?
  • a cartoon cat?
  • a sleeping cat under a blanket?

You quickly realise you can't write rules for every possible cat. The real world is too messy, variable, and ambiguous.

This was the wall AI researchers hit for decades. Machine learning was the breakthrough — instead of programmers writing every rule by hand, computers could learn the patterns from data themselves.
Section 03

Making Sense of the Jargon

The terms AI, machine learning, and generative AI get thrown around constantly and used interchangeably — but they mean different things. Think of them as nested concepts, like Russian dolls.

Artificial Intelligence (AI)

The broadest term — computer systems that perform tasks normally associated with human intelligence: recognising patterns, making predictions, understanding language, making judgements. Unlike traditional software that just follows fixed instructions, AI systems adapt their behaviour based on data or context. Spam filters, satnav route planning, chess computers, and Netflix recommendations are all examples of AI. It's like saying "medicine" covers everything from a GP visit to neurosurgery.

Machine Learning (ML)

A subset of AI where the computer learns from data rather than being explicitly programmed with rules. Instead of a human writing IF/THEN logic, you give the system thousands of examples and it figures out the patterns itself.

Deep Learning

A subset of machine learning using neural networks with many stacked layers (hence "deep"). This is what powers facial recognition, self-driving cars, language translation, and AlphaFold — the AI that solved protein structure prediction.

Generative AI

A subset of deep learning that creates new content — text, images, video, music, code — rather than just analysing or classifying existing data. Traditional ML looks at an email and says "spam" or "not spam." Generative AI writes you a brand new email from scratch.

Large Language Models (LLMs)

A specific type of generative AI that has read an enormous amount of human writing — books, articles, websites, conversations — and learned the patterns of how language works. When you type a question, it generates a response by predicting what words should come next, drawing on everything it has read. ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are all LLMs.

When someone says "AI" in 2026 they usually mean generative AI — specifically large language models like ChatGPT and Claude.
A Concrete Example

Imagine you want AI to help with patient letters. A traditional AI system auto-fills template letters based on diagnosis codes — rigid but technically AI. A machine learning system trained on thousands of past letters predicts which template to use. A generative AI system lets you describe the encounter in a few lines and writes a complete, personalised discharge letter in appropriate clinical language.

Section 04

How Machine Learning Works

The Key Insight — Let the Computer Figure It Out

Instead of the programmer writing rules, you give the computer thousands of examples and let it figure out the rules itself.

Think of how a child learns language — nobody teaches a toddler the rules of grammar. They hear thousands of hours of people talking and gradually pick up the patterns. Machine learning is the same idea applied to computers.

And remember the cat recognition problem? You don't write rules at all. You show the system ten thousand photos labelled "cat" and ten thousand labelled "not cat" and it figures out for itself what makes a cat a cat.

The Video Game Example

In 2013, DeepMind built an AI system and pointed it at classic Atari games — Pong, Breakout, Space Invaders. The system was given the pixels on screen, the available controls, and a score. No rules. No strategy. No explanation of what the game even was.

It started playing randomly, mashing buttons. But every time it scored a point it learned "do more of that." Within hours it was beating human players at Pong. With Breakout it figured out on its own that if you tunnel the ball behind the wall of bricks it bounces around destroying everything. Nobody programmed that strategy.

Move 37 — The Moment AI Shocked the World

In 2016 DeepMind's AlphaGo took on Lee Sedol — one of the greatest Go players in history. Chess computers had beaten humans in 1997 but Go was considered decades away from being solved — too intuitive, too creative, too human.

In Game Two, AlphaGo played Move 37 — a move so unusual that commentators thought it was a mistake. No human would have played it. It broke every conventional rule of Go strategy.

It won the game. And ultimately the match, 4 games to 1. Move 37 became iconic because it wasn't just the computer being faster — it was being creative in a way humans didn't expect.

Why It Took So Long

The concept has been around since the 1950s but for decades we didn't have enough data to train on or enough computing power to process it. The field went through several "AI winters" — periods of hype followed by disappointment when the technology couldn't deliver.

Section 05

Neural Networks & Deep Learning

What is a Neural Network?

Systems loosely inspired by how the brain works. Instead of one big algorithm you have layers of simple processing units connected together — a bit like neurons. Data goes in one end, passes through layers, and a result comes out the other end.

How Training Works

Each connection between layers has a weight — a number saying how important that signal is. Training = adjusting millions of these weights until the network gets accurate.

  1. Show the network an example (eg a photo of a cat)
  2. It gets the answer wrong
  3. Adjust the weights slightly
  4. Repeat millions of times
  5. Eventually it becomes eerily accurate

Conceptually similar to learning darts. Your first throw misses. Your brain adjusts. After thousands of throws you're hitting the bullseye — not because someone told you the physics, but because you've made tiny corrections thousands of times.

Deep Learning (2012 onwards)

When you stack many layers of networks on top of each other = deep learning. Things got serious around 2012 when a deep learning model smashed existing records in image recognition.

GPUs — chips originally designed for gaming — turned out to be perfect for training because they can do millions of small calculations simultaneously.

The Black Box Problem

Here's a strange thing about neural networks: even the people who build them don't fully understand how they work.

A modern AI model contains billions of connection weights, all adjusted automatically during training. When the model gives you an answer, it isn't following a set of rules a human wrote. It's running a calculation across all those billions of weights in a way no human can meaningfully read or trace. You can see the input. You can see the output. But what happens in between — why the model chose those particular words — is largely opaque, even to its creators.

This is called the black box problem. The model works, but it can't easily explain itself, and we can't easily look inside.

It has real consequences:

  • When an AI gets something wrong, it's hard to know why — so it's hard to guarantee it won't happen again
  • When an AI is biased, the bias is buried in the weights rather than written into rules you can find and fix
  • When an AI gets something surprisingly right (like Move 37), nobody can fully explain how it got there either

A whole field of research called interpretability is trying to peek inside the box and understand what's happening. Progress is being made, but for now, modern AI remains one of the strangest tools humans have ever built: something we can create, train, and use — but not fully explain.

The Language Problem

Despite progress in image recognition, language remained the tough nut to crack. Models processed text sequentially — one word at a time, left to right.

Like trying to understand a novel by reading it through a letterbox — one word at a time, trying to remember everything.
Section 06

The Transformer — The Breakthrough

"Attention Is All You Need" (Google, 2017)

This is genuinely the single most important development in the entire AI story. A team at Google introduced a new architecture called the transformer — arguably the most consequential computer science paper of the 21st century.

The Attention Mechanism

Instead of reading text one word at a time, the transformer looks at an entire passage simultaneously and figures out which words relate to which, regardless of distance.

Analogy — The Dinner Party

Old approach: Blindfolded at a dinner party, only able to hear the person directly beside you, one at a time, going around the table.

Transformer: Taking the blindfold off — suddenly you can see everyone, follow multiple conversations at once, understand who is responding to whom even across the table.

What This Enabled

  • Grasp that "bank" means different things in "river bank" vs "bank account"
  • Follow complex references and pronouns
  • Track the thread of an argument across paragraphs
Every single major AI model — ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok, Llama — is built on this transformer architecture.

Why It Matters to You

You don't need to understand the maths — but knowing this architecture exists explains why these models are so good at language and why they all emerged around the same time. Every company is building on the same fundamental breakthrough. The competition is in how they train the models, what data they use, and how they fine-tune the behaviour.

Section 07

Scaling Laws

The Three Ingredients

After the transformer was invented, researchers started making models bigger and discovered something remarkable. Scale up three things and performance improves dramatically and predictably:

  1. Compute — more powerful hardware to train on
  2. Data — more text and examples for the model to learn from
  3. Parameters — making the model itself larger (more connection weights = bigger brain)

These are called scaling laws — first formally described by OpenAI researchers around 2020.

Parameters vs Compute — The Engine Analogy

Two of these ideas get confused constantly. They are related — but they are not the same thing.

Parameters = The Size of the Engine

Parameters are the adjustable connection weights inside the model's neural network. Think of them as the number of connections in a brain — or the size of an engine in a car. More parameters means more capacity to store patterns, relationships, and knowledge.

The humans who design the model decide its architecture — how many layers, how many connections between them, how they're arranged. But the values of those connection weights aren't set by humans. They start as random numbers, and during training the AI adjusts every single one — often billions of them — until the model gets accurate. The designers build the engine; the training process tunes it.

Compute = The Fuel and Horsepower

Compute is the computational power used to train and run the model. Think of it as the fuel, the electricity, the horsepower needed to make the engine work. Even a huge engine is useless without enough fuel behind it. A massive AI model cannot function effectively without enormous computational resources.

In practice, this compute comes from specialised chips called GPUs — originally designed for video games but ideal for the massive parallel calculations AI training requires.

Why This Matters

The improvement is not marginal — it is dramatic. You can draw a graph and reliably predict how much better a model will get if you double the compute or data.

This is why companies like NVIDIA became so important — their GPUs are exceptionally good at the massive parallel calculations needed for AI training. It is also why companies are building enormous data centres filled with specialised AI hardware, and why there is talk of spending hundreds of billions on AI infrastructure.

Nobody has hit a ceiling yet.

The Debate

There is genuine debate about whether this continues indefinitely. Some researchers think we're approaching limits (running out of quality data, enormous energy costs). Others think we'll find ways around these constraints (synthetic data, more efficient architectures). What's undeniable is that scaling has worked remarkably well so far.

Section 08

How AI Models Are Trained

So far we've talked about the ingredients of modern AI: neural networks, the transformer, scaling. But how does a blank network full of random numbers actually become ChatGPT? The answer is a three-stage training process.

Stage 1 — Pre-training

This is the big, expensive step. The model is shown an enormous amount of text — books, articles, websites, code, transcripts — and asked to do one thing over and over: predict the next word.

Given "The cat sat on the…" the model guesses. At first the guesses are random. The training process compares the guess to the real next word, and adjusts the billions of connection weights very slightly to make a better guess next time. Repeat trillions of times across trillions of words and the model gradually learns the patterns of language — grammar, facts, reasoning, style, and a great deal of world knowledge.

At the end of pre-training the model is fluent and knowledgeable. But it doesn't know how to be useful. Ask it a question and it might answer, or it might continue the question, or generate a list of similar questions, or wander off — because in its training data, all of those were valid continuations.

Stage 2 — Fine-tuning

Next, the model is shown thousands of carefully written examples of what a good response looks like: a question paired with an ideal answer, an instruction paired with a faithful completion. This teaches the model the format of being an assistant — to answer questions when asked, follow instructions when given, and stay on topic.

Fine-tuning is much smaller and cheaper than pre-training. It doesn't add new knowledge so much as shape how the model uses what it already learned.

Stage 3 — RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)

The final stage is where the model learns to be genuinely helpful. Human raters look at thousands of pairs of model responses and pick which one they prefer — more accurate, more helpful, more honest, less harmful. The model adjusts itself based on these preferences and gradually starts producing the kind of answers humans actually want.

Because hiring enough humans to do this at full scale is impossible, a second AI is then trained on the human preferences and takes over much of the rating work. The model keeps learning.

This is why ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini all feel polite, careful, and broadly aligned with what you're trying to do — even though the underlying language models are next-word predictors at heart. Most of the personality and helpfulness you experience is the result of RLHF.

Pre-training teaches the model language. Fine-tuning teaches it to answer. RLHF teaches it to be helpful.

Why This Takes So Much Compute

Pre-training a frontier model can cost tens or hundreds of millions of dollars and run on tens of thousands of GPUs for weeks or months at a time. This is why the scaling laws from the previous section matter so much — more compute, more data, more parameters all feed directly into this pre-training stage.

Fine-tuning and RLHF are far cheaper but still substantial. By the time a model reaches you through a chat window, it has been through one of the most computationally intensive processes humans have ever undertaken.

The Limits and Biases of Training

Training a model on enormous amounts of human-produced text and refining it with human preferences has an important consequence: the model inherits the worldview of its training data and the people who shaped it.

Bias can creep in at every stage:

  • From the data itself. If the model is trained mostly on English-language text from the internet, it will reflect the assumptions, cultural references, and blind spots of that material. Medical research, news coverage, opinion pieces — they all carry the perspective of their authors and their time.
  • From the humans who fine-tune and rate it. During fine-tuning and RLHF, real people are deciding which answers are "better." Their cultural background, values, and instincts get baked into the model.
  • From the rules the company applies. Every model has guidelines about what topics it will and won't engage with — set by the company that built it.

In practice this means different models have noticeably different personalities and politics. Most of today's frontier models — ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok — are American-made, trained predominantly on English text, and tend to reflect a broadly Western, liberal-democratic worldview.

Chinese models like DeepSeek and Qwen are highly capable but operate under different rules. Ask DeepSeek about Tiananmen Square, the status of Taiwan, or criticisms of the Chinese Communist Party and it will typically refuse to engage, deflect, or repeat the official government position. That isn't a flaw — it's a deliberate design choice, just as American models have their own design choices about what they will and won't say.

No AI model is a neutral oracle. Every model has a point of view — shaped by who built it, what data they used, and what rules they applied. Useful, yes. Impartial, no.
Section 09

Where We Are Now

Reasoning — AI That Actually Thinks

Early models (GPT-3, early ChatGPT) were sophisticated pattern completers. Brilliant at producing fluent text — but not actually thinking. They would confidently give wrong answers to maths problems in beautiful prose.

Critics called these models "stochastic parrots" — a phrase from a 2021 paper (Bender et al.) arguing they were sophisticated mimics, randomly stitching together patterns without understanding. Like a parrot saying "Polly wants a cracker" perfectly but having no idea what a cracker is.

The latest generation has changed the conversation with reasoning:

  • Models can work through problems step by step
  • Plan approaches, consider alternatives, check their own work, catch mistakes
The Shift

Early models = a very articulate person who sometimes talks nonsense with complete confidence.

Reasoning models = someone who actually stops to think before they speak.

Multimodal — Beyond Text

Models have gone multimodal — they no longer just work with text.

  • See and understand images — upload a photo and ask questions
  • Generate images from text descriptions
  • Understand and transcribe audio
  • Generate video from text prompts
  • Create music — vocals, instruments, full production
  • Read and analyse documents — PDFs, spreadsheets, presentations
  • Write and run code — and execute it to check if it works

Agents — AI That Takes Action

The next frontier — we are right at the beginning of this. AI is moving from answering questions to doing things:

  • Browse the web for you
  • Use software on your behalf
  • Manage files and organise information
  • Handle multi-step workflows autonomously
The shift from "AI answers my questions" to "AI does my tasks" is the biggest change coming.
Section 10

Under the Hood

You don't need to understand the engineering — but knowing a few concepts will make you a much better user.

Tokens — How AI Reads

AI models don't read words the way you do. They break text into tokens — chunks roughly three-quarters of a word on average.

  • "dermatology" = roughly 3 tokens
  • "the cat sat on the mat" = 6 tokens
  • A page of text ≈ 300–400 tokens

Why this matters: usage limits are measured in tokens. Pricing on the API is per token. A "128k context window" means roughly 100,000 words — about a 300-page book.

The Context Window — AI's Working Memory

The total amount of text the model can "see" at once — your conversation history, any uploaded documents, and its own responses.

Think of it like a desk. Everything the AI is working with has to fit on the desk. Once the desk is full, the oldest material falls off the edge.

This is why very long conversations can lose coherence, why starting a new chat for a new topic is often better, and why uploading documents works so well — it puts the source material directly on the desk.

Temperature — Creativity vs Consistency

When AI generates text it doesn't just pick the single most likely next word every time. Temperature controls how much randomness is in the selection.

  • Low temperature = predictable, focused. Good for factual work, data analysis, coding
  • High temperature = creative, varied. Good for brainstorming, creative writing

The Knowledge Cutoff

AI models are trained on data up to a certain date — their knowledge cutoff. They don't automatically know about events after that date. Most models now have web search built in to compensate — but you should always be cautious about time-sensitive information.

Section 11

The Frontier AI Models

Most people interacting with AI today are using a frontier model — one of a small handful of cutting-edge systems built by major AI companies. Understanding the landscape briefly will help you choose what to use.

Open Source vs Closed Source

AI models come in two broad varieties.

Closed-source models are owned and operated by a single company. You can only access them through that company's app, website, or API — the model itself is locked away. ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are all closed-source. The advantage is polish, support, and the most cutting-edge capabilities. The disadvantage is you're entirely dependent on the company: their pricing, their privacy policies, their decisions about what the model will and won't do.

Open-source models are released publicly, often with the full weights of the model available for anyone to download, modify, and run themselves. Meta (Facebook's parent company) is the biggest player here — their Llama family of models is freely available and widely used. Mistral (a French company) and DeepSeek (Chinese) also release their model weights this way.

Strictly speaking, most of these are better described as "open-weights" rather than fully open-source — the model itself is downloadable, but the training data and full recipe usually aren't. Still, the practical effect is the same: anyone with enough computing power can run these models privately, on their own hardware, without sending data to a third party. For privacy-sensitive work, they're an important option.

The Four Main Closed-Source Frontier Models

Four companies currently dominate the closed-source frontier:

  • ChatGPT — OpenAI
  • Claude — Anthropic
  • Gemini — Google
  • Grok — xAI

Despite the marketing, they share most of their core capabilities.

What They All Can Do

Every frontier model today is far more than the original chatbot ChatGPT launched as in 2022. All four can:

  • Hold a natural conversation, answer questions, write and edit text in any style
  • Search the web in real time and cite sources
  • Generate images from text descriptions
  • Generate or edit short video clips
  • Have a spoken conversation through their mobile apps — surprisingly natural
  • See and understand images you upload — diagrams, photos, screenshots
  • Read and analyse documents — PDFs, spreadsheets, presentations
  • Translate between languages fluently

Where They're Heading — Useful Agents

The next leap — already well underway — is models that don't just answer you but actually do things on your behalf. Modern frontier models can increasingly:

  • Read and write files on your computer — open a spreadsheet, edit a document, save a report
  • Write code by talking to them in plain English — describe what you want and watch them build it
  • Connect to other software — your calendar, email, project tools, PowerPoint, Excel, Drive, and many more — through what are called integrations or connectors
  • Use a web browser on your behalf — book a flight, fill in a form, do research

This shift from "AI that answers questions" to "AI that takes actions" is the biggest change happening right now, and all four frontier models are racing to make it work well.

Free vs Paid — The General Picture

Every frontier model offers both a free tier and a paid tier (usually around €20/month).

The free tier gives you a taste — but typically uses older or smaller models, with stricter limits on how much you can use it each day, and reduced access to the newer capabilities like image generation, file analysis, and web search.

The paid tier gives you the current best model from that company, far more usage, and full access to features like file uploads, image and video generation, voice mode, and the new agentic capabilities described above. For most people who use AI more than occasionally, the paid tier pays for itself many times over in time saved.

A more detailed breakdown of which model to choose, and the specific differences between them, will follow in a separate guide.

Section 12

MCQ Flashcards

Test yourself on sections 1–11. Tap an option to check your answer, or reveal it directly.

A · Foundations
B · How AI Was Built
C · Training & Bias
D · Models & Today
Q1Which of these best describes traditional software?
  • AIt learns from examples rather than being given rules
  • BIt can adapt when situations fall outside its instructions
  • CIt follows rules a human has written, exactly and without question
  • DIt has its own understanding and judgement
C — Traditional software follows the rules a human wrote, with no judgement of its own.
Q2Why does rule-based programming struggle with problems like recognising a cat in a photo?
  • AComputers cannot process images
  • BThe variation in real-world cats is too messy to capture in IF/THEN rules
  • CPhotos are too large for traditional software to handle
  • DCat recognition requires internet access
B — Real-world cats vary too much (angles, lighting, breeds, cartoons) to cover with hand-written rules.
Q3How do AI, machine learning, deep learning, and generative AI relate to each other?
  • AThey are unrelated fields that happen to share names
  • BThey are different names for the same technology
  • CThey are nested — each is a subset of the broader one
  • DDeep learning is the broadest term, with AI as a subset
C — Generative AI ⊂ Deep Learning ⊂ Machine Learning ⊂ AI. Russian nesting dolls.
Q4What is the key difference between traditional machine learning and generative AI?
  • AGenerative AI is faster than traditional ML
  • BTraditional ML analyses or classifies; generative AI creates new content
  • CGenerative AI only works with text, traditional ML works with everything else
  • DTraditional ML requires more data than generative AI
B — Traditional ML decides what existing data is (e.g. "spam or not spam"); generative AI produces new content.
Q5What does LLM stand for?
  • ALogical Learning Machine
  • BLinear Language Method
  • CLarge Language Model
  • DLow-Latency Module
C — Large Language Model. ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are all LLMs.
Q6Which best describes how an LLM produces a response?
  • AIt searches the web and copies the relevant text
  • BIt looks up the answer in a database of facts
  • CIt runs the question through a set of human-written rules
  • DIt predicts what words should come next, based on patterns learned from vast amounts of text
D — At their core, LLMs are next-word predictors trained on enormous amounts of human writing.
Q7What was remarkable about DeepMind's AI playing Breakout?
  • AIt was the first computer ever to play a video game
  • BIt discovered the tunnelling strategy by itself, without being taught
  • CIt used a human's playbook of strategies
  • DIt rewrote the game's source code
B — Nobody programmed the tunnelling strategy. It learned it through trial and error.
Q8Why was "Move 37" in AlphaGo vs Lee Sedol so significant?
  • AIt was the move that cost AlphaGo the game
  • BIt was a standard opening that any expert would have played
  • CIt was a creative move no human would have played — and it worked
  • DIt was the fastest move in Go history
C — Commentators thought it was a mistake. It turned out to be a creative move that won the game.
Q9What does the "black box problem" refer to?
  • AAI models running on hidden hardware
  • BEven the people who build AI models can't fully explain how they reach specific answers
  • CAI being unable to answer in plain language
  • DThe cost of AI training being kept secret
B — The model works, but its reasoning is buried in billions of weights and largely opaque even to its creators.
Q10What does the research field of "interpretability" try to do?
  • AUnderstand what's happening inside neural networks
  • BTranslate between human languages
  • CMake AI models faster
  • DReduce the energy cost of training
A — Interpretability is the field trying to peek inside the black box.
Q11What was the breakthrough idea of the transformer architecture in 2017?
  • AReading text one word at a time, more efficiently
  • BUsing larger databases
  • CThe attention mechanism — looking at an entire passage simultaneously and figuring out which words relate to which
  • DConnecting AI to the internet
C — Attention lets the model see the whole passage at once, like taking the blindfold off at a dinner party.
Q12What is the title of the 2017 Google paper that introduced the transformer?
  • A"Deep Learning for Language"
  • B"Attention Is All You Need"
  • C"The Future of AI"
  • D"Beyond Recurrent Networks"
B — "Attention Is All You Need" is arguably the most consequential computer science paper of the 21st century.
Q13Every major AI model today (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok, Llama) is built on which architecture?
  • AThe transformer
  • BRecurrent neural networks
  • CDecision trees
  • DRule-based logic engines
A — All frontier LLMs are transformer-based. The competition is in training, data, and fine-tuning.
Q14What are the three components of "scaling laws"?
  • AHardware, software, internet
  • BCompute, data, parameters
  • CSpeed, memory, storage
  • DText, images, video
B — Scale up compute, data, and parameters and performance improves predictably.
Q15Using the engine analogy, what are parameters?
  • AThe fuel that powers the engine
  • BThe road the engine drives on
  • CThe size of the engine — how many connections it has
  • DThe driver controlling the engine
C — Parameters are the model's connection weights. More parameters = larger engine.
Q16Who decides the values of a model's connection weights?
  • AThe human designers set them by hand
  • BThe designers choose the architecture; the training process tunes the weights
  • CThey are randomly generated and never adjusted
  • DUsers adjust them by interacting with the model
B — Humans design the architecture (layers, connections); training adjusts the billions of weight values.
Q17What are GPUs and why do they matter for AI?
  • ASpecialised chips originally designed for video games, ideal for the parallel calculations AI training needs
  • BSoftware programs that run AI models
  • CDatasets used to train language models
  • DThe user interface of AI chatbots
A — GPUs do millions of small calculations in parallel, which turns out to be perfect for AI training.
Q18What are the three main stages of training a modern AI model?
  • AProgramming, testing, deployment
  • BData collection, encryption, release
  • CPre-training, fine-tuning, RLHF
  • DCompilation, optimisation, distribution
C — Pre-training (learn language), fine-tuning (learn to answer), RLHF (learn to be helpful).
Q19What does RLHF stand for, and what does it do?
  • ARandom Language Hyper-Filtering — removes inappropriate content
  • BReinforcement Learning from Human Feedback — teaches the model which kinds of answers humans prefer
  • CRecursive Logic Heuristic Framework — improves reasoning
  • DReal-time Live Hosting Function — connects model to the web
B — RLHF is what makes ChatGPT feel polite and helpful rather than like a raw next-word predictor.
Q20According to the guide, which of these is NOT a way that bias can enter an AI model?
  • AThe training data carries the perspectives of its authors
  • BThe humans rating responses during RLHF have their own values
  • CThe company applies rules about what the model will and won't discuss
  • DThe model develops its own political views during conversations
D — Bias enters through data, raters, and company rules — not through real-time opinion-forming.
Q21What is likely to happen if you ask DeepSeek about Tiananmen Square?
  • AIt will give an objective historical account
  • BIt will hallucinate confidently
  • CIt will refuse, deflect, or repeat the official Chinese government position
  • DIt will redirect you to ChatGPT
C — Chinese models operate under different rules. This is a deliberate design choice, not a bug.
Q22What did critics mean by calling early language models "stochastic parrots"?
  • AThey could mimic bird sounds
  • BThey were sophisticated mimics of patterns without genuine understanding
  • CThey learned from random data
  • DThey were colourful and unpredictable
B — The critique: very articulate, no real comprehension. Modern reasoning models complicate this picture.
Q23What is "hallucination" in AI?
  • AWhen an AI generates an image
  • BWhen an AI gives confident-sounding answers that are factually wrong
  • CWhen an AI refuses to answer
  • DWhen an AI slows down under heavy load
B — Hallucination is fluent, confident nonsense. Always verify high-stakes answers.
Q24What does "multimodal" mean for modern AI?
  • AIt runs on multiple devices at once
  • BIt has different pricing tiers
  • CIt can work with multiple types of input and output — text, images, audio, video
  • DIt connects to multiple websites
C — The walls between media types are dissolving — one model can see, hear, speak, and generate visuals.
Q25What is an AI "agent" in current development?
  • AA human employee using AI
  • BAn AI system that takes actions on your behalf — files, browsing, software
  • CA customer support chatbot
  • DAn AI representative for a company
B — The shift from "AI that answers questions" to "AI that does tasks" is the biggest change happening right now.
Q26What is the "context window" of an AI model?
  • AThe screen size needed to use the model
  • BThe total amount of text the model can see and work with at once
  • CThe time period the model was trained on
  • DThe number of users supported simultaneously
B — Think of it as a desk: everything in the conversation has to fit on it, or it falls off the edge.
Q27What is a "token" in an AI model?
  • AA subscription credit
  • BA type of password
  • CA chunk of text — roughly three-quarters of a word on average
  • DA single image pixel
C — Models read in tokens rather than words. Usage limits and pricing are measured in tokens.
Q28What is the difference between closed-source and open-source AI models?
  • AClosed-source is locked to one company's app; open-source can be downloaded and run yourself
  • BClosed-source costs money; open-source is free to use
  • CClosed-source is American; open-source is European
  • DThere is no real difference
A — Open-source/open-weights models like Llama or DeepSeek can be downloaded and run privately on your own hardware.
Q29Which company is the biggest player in open-source AI?
  • AOpenAI
  • BMeta (with the Llama family)
  • CGoogle
  • DAnthropic
B — Meta's Llama models are the biggest open-weights family. Mistral and DeepSeek are also notable.
Q30Which of these is NOT one of the four main closed-source frontier models?
  • AChatGPT
  • BClaude
  • CGemini
  • DLlama
D — Llama is Meta's open-source family. The four closed-source frontier models are ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Grok.
